perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto shall limit access to the other party’s Proprietary Information to those of its employees, agents, advisors, and consultants, who have a reasonable need for access to such information in connection with the discussion and exploration of the Purpose and who shall be subject to the non-disclosure covenants contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto shall limit access to the other party’s Proprietary Information to any individual, regardless of their relationship to the company, who may potentially contribute to the discussion and exploration of the Purpose, and who may be subject to the non-disclosure covenants contained herein.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'employees, agents, advisors, and consultants' to 'any individual' broadens the scope significantly. This potentially includes individuals without a direct contractual relationship or fiduciary duty, weakening the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows access to 'any individual' which means people without any obligation to maintain confidentiality. This makes it easier for the information to be exposed, misappropriated, or disclosed unlawfully, hindering the protection of the trade secret as provided for under 18 U.S. Code § 1832.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The original contract limits access to proprietary information to specific individuals (employees, agents, advisors, and consultants) bound by non-disclosure covenants. The modified text broadens this to 'any individual,' regardless of their relationship to the company. This change creates a legal contradiction because it includes individuals who may not be subject to the same contractual or fiduciary duties, thus weakening the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement and potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets, as suggested by the Law Explanation referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1832 (though no specific text from that code is provided in the scraped snippets).

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of laws provisions within.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the whims of the involved parties, without regard to any specific jurisdiction or legal framework.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'laws of the State of New York' with 'whims of the involved parties' removes the legal basis for interpreting and enforcing the contract, making it unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts established contract law principles, specifically New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401, which allows parties to choose New York law to govern contracts exceeding a certain value. By stating the agreement is governed by 'whims,' it negates this right and renders the contract legally ambiguous and unenforceable under New York law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401, as outlined in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original text specifies adherence to New York law, while the changed text replaces this with 'whims of the involved parties,' explicitly rejecting any specific jurisdiction or legal framework. This negates the parties' ability to choose New York law to govern their contract, a right provided under GOB § 5-1401, rendering the contract legally ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto shall take reasonable and necessary measures to preserve the secrecy and confidentiality and avoid the unauthorized use or disclosure of the other party’s Proprietary Information, including, without limitation, taking such measures of protection as it takes to protect its own confidential, proprietary or trade secret information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto may, at their discretion, take limited and potentially insufficient measures to preserve the secrecy and confidentiality, or potentially allow the unauthorized use or disclosure of the other party’s Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes an obligation to take reasonable and necessary measures. The modified text makes this discretionary and allows for potentially insufficient measures, which undermines the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By allowing 'limited and potentially insufficient measures,' the modified text contradicts the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, thus failing to protect the 'proprietary information'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Trade Secrets Act - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Uniform act in the United States Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e The Uniform Trade Secrets Act ( UTSA ), published by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) in 1979 and amended in 1985, is a model law designed for adoption by U.S. states . [ 1 ] It was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states. [ 1 ] (p1) The UTSA provides unitary definitions for "trade secret" and "trade secret misappropriation" and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property, quasi-contractual, and fiduciary liability frameworks previously applied under common law . [ 1 ] (p2) To date, 49 states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have adopted the UTSA, with New York as the remaining exceptions. [ 2 ] Motivation [ edit ] A prefatory note to the UTSA states some original motivations for the act: A valid patent provides a legal monopoly for seventeen years in exchange for public disclosure of an invention. If, however, the courts ultimately decide that the Patent Office improperly issued a patent, an invention has been disclosed to competitors with no corresponding benefit. In view of the substantial number of patents that the courts invalidate, many businesses now elect to protect commercially valuable information by relying on the state trade secret protection law. — Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Prefatory Note [ 3 ] The UTSA made note of the commercial value and competitive advantages inherent in trade secrets. Unlike patent protection, which was addressed at the federal level, trade secret misappropriation was addressed at the state level. [ 3 ] In the United States there existed a prevalence of interstate commercial transactions that extended beyond the jurisdiction of individual state legislation. For example, goods may have been manufactured in State A, warehoused in State B, sold from State C, and delivered in State D. As a result, the UTSA sought to alleviate the uneven development and "uncertainty concerning the parameters of trade secret protection" by recommending a uniform trade secret law and, at the same time, allowing the states the flexibility to meet local circumstances by modifying the text as enacted in eac
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the requirement for reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, as implied by the UTSA. The original text mandates 'reasonable and necessary measures,' while the changed text allows for 'limited and potentially insufficient measures,' explicitly undermining the protection of proprietary information, which is contrary to the intent of trade secret law to protect proprietary information. The Law Explanation states that by allowing 'limited and potentially insufficient measures,' the modified text contradicts the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Snippet 2 states that the UTSA was developed to resolve inconsistencies in the treatment of trade secrets across different states and establishes a single statute of limitations, replacing the varied property frameworks previously applied under common law. This implies a standard of care, which the changed text does not meet.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, OCERA and COMPANY may each retain one (1) copy of such information in their confidential files solely for record keeping purposes to ensure compliance with this Agreement, with such copy remaining subject to the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, OCERA and COMPANY may each distribute multiple copies of such information throughout their internal networks for vague archival reasons, with such copies no longer bound by the confidentiality obligations outlined in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows one copy for record keeping, bound by confidentiality. The modified text allows multiple copies distributed throughout internal networks, no longer bound by confidentiality. This directly contradicts the original intent and weakens protection of proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Distributing multiple copies without confidentiality obligations significantly increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation, violating the spirit and potentially the letter of the Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S.C. § 1831), which aims to protect proprietary economic information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality , or foreign agent, knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret ; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys a trade secret ; (3) receives, buys, or possesses a trade secret , knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined not more than $5,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 15 years, or both. (b) Organizations.— Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $10,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3488 ; amended Pub. L. 112–269, § 2 , Jan. 14, 2013 , 126 Stat. 2442 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2013—Subsec. (a). Pub. L. 112–269, § 2(a) , substituted “not more than $5,000,000” for “not more than $500,000” in concluding provisions. Subsec. (b). Pub. L. 112–2
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement by allowing multiple copies of confidential information to be distributed throughout internal networks without confidentiality obligations, where the original text limited it to one copy for record keeping with confidentiality obligations. This creates a significant risk of trade secret misappropriation, potentially violating 18 U.S.C. § 1831, which addresses the unauthorized copying, duplication, or communication of a trade secret. The scraped law snippet states "Whoever... without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys a trade secret" could face legal repercussions. By removing the confidentiality obligations and allowing multiple copies, the change increases the likelihood of unauthorized distribution and use, therefore creating a contradiction.
